news_summary: Experts debunk common language learning myths while innovative art transforms Paris’s Pont Neuf with a striking inflatable installation.
news_summary_fr: Des experts démystifient les idées reçues sur l'apprentissage des langues, tandis qu'une œuvre d'art innovante transforme le Pont-Neuf à Paris grâce à une impressionnante installation gonflable.
news_summary_es: Los expertos desmontan los mitos más comunes sobre el aprendizaje de idiomas, mientras que una innovadora obra de arte transforma el Pont Neuf de París con una llamativa instalación hinchable.

articles.title: Think you’re bad at languages? Experts say these 5 myths are to blame
articles.summary: Many people avoid learning a new language because they remember stressful grammar lessons or fear making mistakes. But language experts say communication, culture, and connection matter far more than perfection. Modern apps, entertainment, travel, and online communities have made learning easier, more social, and surprisingly fun.
articles.content:
Language learning is often a daunting prospect. Many of us wish we had learned a language to a higher level at school. But even though adults of all ages can do well in acquiring a new language, fear – or the memory of struggling to memorize grammar at school – can hold us back.

We both work in languages education and recognize the real benefits that learning another language can bring. As well as myriad cognitive benefits, it brings with it cultural insights and empathetic awareness.

With that in mind, we’re here to dispel five myths about language learning that might be putting you off.

Myth one: it’s all about grammar and vocabulary

In fact, learning about people, history and culture is arguably the best part of learning a language. While grammar and vocabulary are undeniably important aspects of language learning, they don’t exist in isolation from how people communicate in everyday life.

Language learning can help us to have “intercultural agility”: the ability to engage empathically with people who have very different experiences from our own. To be able to do this means learning about people, history and culture.

Immersing yourself in a particular country or location, for example through studying or working, is a fantastic way to do this. But when this isn’t feasible, there are so many other options available. We can learn so much through music, books, films, musical theater and gaming.

Myth two: we should focus on avoiding mistakes – they’re embarrassing

One problem with formal language learning is that it encourages us to focus on accuracy at all costs. To pass exams, you need to get things “right”. And many of us feel nervous about getting things wrong.

But in real-life communication, even in our expert languages, we often make mistakes and get away with it. Think of the number of times you have misspelled something, or said the wrong word, and still been understood.

Less formal language learning can encourage us to think more about communication than accuracy.

One advocate of this approach is author Benny Lewis, who popularized a communicative learning approach he calls “language hacking” which focuses on the language skills needed for conversation. Language apps also encourage this, as does real-life travel and communication.

Myth three: it’s too much effort to start over with a new language

You can use languages in lots of ways, and the language you learn at school doesn’t have to be the only one you learn.

In England, most people learn one or more of French, Spanish or German at school. These languages can often serve as great apprenticeship languages, teaching us how to learn a language and about grammatical structures.

But they are not always the languages that we are most likely to use as adults, when family and work could take us anywhere. Our cultural interests might also lead us to want to know more about a new language.

Learning a language that you have a personal interest in can be very motivating and help you to keep going when things get a bit rocky.

Myth four: learning a language is an individual endeavor

You don’t have to learn alone. Learning with others, or having the support of others, can help motivate us to learn.

This might be through a multilingual marriage, joining a conversation group or chatting in a language learning forum online. Don’t feel that you have to have reached a certain proficiency before you start reaching out to others.

Language apps can also make language learning a collective endeavor. You can learn along with friends and family, and congratulate them on their language learning streaks.

This is something both of us do with multiple generations of our families, helping us engage with language learning in a lighthearted way.

Myth five: it’s a lot of hard graft

Learning a language in a systematic way can be challenging, whether in a classroom or from a self-study course. But some things make this easier. We have found that people are more motivated to engage when they have a personal reason to learn. This could be, for example, wanting to communicate with family or to travel to a particular country or region.

The growth in popularity and accessibility of language learning apps has made language learning possible from any location and at any time, often for free.

You can easily catch up on your Chinese from the comfort of your own armchair, at whatever time is most convenient for you. Apps can be fun and playful, and can help us maintain motivation, develop vocabulary and embed grammatical structures.

There are lots of reasons for learning a language, and lots of benefits. We encourage everyone to focus on these benefits, and give it a go.
articles.reasoning: The article promotes language learning by dispelling common myths, highlighting cognitive, cultural, and social benefits, and emphasizing accessible modern methods like apps and community support. This has broad positive impact by encouraging personal growth, intercultural empathy, and lifelong learning for a wide audience.
articles.personality_title: Five myths that stop people from learning new languages, explained
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people avoid learning new languages because they remember difficult grammar lessons or worry about making mistakes. This can make language learning seem hard and scary.

**What happened** – Language experts explained five common myths that discourage people from trying to learn languages. They said language learning is not just about grammar and vocabulary, but also about understanding culture and communicating with others. They also pointed out that mistakes are normal, learning can be fun with apps and games, and you don’t have to do it alone.

**Impact** – This new way of thinking makes language learning feel more possible and enjoyable for everyone. It shows that you don’t need to be perfect to communicate, and that learning languages can help you connect with people and cultures in real life. It also highlights the many tools available today, like apps and online groups, that make learning easier and more social.

**What’s next step** – People interested in learning a language can start by choosing one that excites them, using apps or joining conversation groups. Schools and educators might also shift focus from strict grammar to more practical and cultural learning, encouraging more people to give languages a try.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Learning a language is less about perfect grammar and more about connecting with others, and today’s tools make it easier and more fun than ever.
articles.personality_title_fr: Cinq idées reçues qui empêchent d’apprendre une langue expliquées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes évitent d’apprendre une nouvelle langue parce qu’elles se souviennent des cours de grammaire difficiles ou ont peur de faire des erreurs. Cela rend l’apprentissage des langues intimidant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des experts en langues ont expliqué cinq mythes courants qui découragent les gens d’apprendre une langue. Ils ont dit que l’apprentissage ne concerne pas seulement la grammaire et le vocabulaire, mais aussi la compréhension de la culture et la communication. Ils ont aussi souligné que faire des erreurs est normal, que l’apprentissage peut être amusant avec des applications et des jeux, et qu’on n’est pas obligé d’apprendre seul.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle façon de penser rend l’apprentissage des langues plus accessible et agréable pour tous. Elle montre qu’il n’est pas nécessaire d’être parfait pour communiquer, et que les langues aident à mieux comprendre les autres et leurs cultures. Elle met aussi en avant les outils modernes comme les applications et les groupes en ligne qui facilitent l’apprentissage.

**Prochaine étape** – Les personnes intéressées peuvent commencer par choisir une langue qui les motive, utiliser des applications ou rejoindre des groupes de conversation. Les écoles peuvent aussi encourager un apprentissage plus pratique et culturel plutôt que centré sur la grammaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Apprendre une langue, c’est moins une question de grammaire parfaite qu’un moyen de se connecter aux autres, et les outils d’aujourd’hui rendent cela plus facile et plus amusant que jamais.
articles.personality_title_es: Cinco mitos que impiden aprender nuevos idiomas, explicados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas evitan aprender un nuevo idioma porque recuerdan las clases de gramática difíciles o tienen miedo de cometer errores. Esto hace que aprender idiomas parezca difícil y aterrador.

**Qué pasó** – Expertos en idiomas explicaron cinco mitos comunes que desaniman a la gente a aprender un idioma. Dijeron que aprender un idioma no es solo gramática y vocabulario, sino también entender la cultura y comunicarse con otros. También señalaron que equivocarse es normal, que aprender puede ser divertido con aplicaciones y juegos, y que no hay que hacerlo solo.

**Impacto** – Esta nueva forma de pensar hace que aprender idiomas sea más posible y agradable para todos. Muestra que no se necesita ser perfecto para comunicarse, y que aprender idiomas ayuda a conectar con personas y culturas reales. Además, destaca las muchas herramientas disponibles hoy, como apps y grupos en línea, que facilitan el aprendizaje y lo hacen más social.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas interesadas pueden empezar eligiendo un idioma que les guste, usando apps o uniéndose a grupos de conversación. Las escuelas y educadores también podrían cambiar el enfoque de la gramática estricta a un aprendizaje más práctico y cultural para animar a más personas a intentarlo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Aprender un idioma es menos sobre gramática perfecta y más sobre conectar con otros, y las herramientas actuales lo hacen más fácil y divertido que nunca.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, warmly lit study scene with an open book displaying diverse cultural icons and language symbols flowing gently like a colorful ribbon connecting to a group of friendly, abstract silhouettes engaged in joyful conversation, surrounded by softly glowing language app icons and musical notes, all rendered in a detailed, painterly style using natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: ‘French Banksy’ JR wraps Paris’s Pont Neuf bridge in inflatable ‘cave’
articles.summary: The Pont Neuf, the oldest bridge in Paris, vanished from sight overnight Thursday as French artist JR, dubbed the "French Banksy", enveloped the historic structure in an inflatable giant cave. The artwork, "La Caverne du Pont Neuf", is a tribute to Parisian artistic legend Christo and his wife, Jeanne-Claude, and will be open to the public from June 6.
articles.content:
Inflation of the artwork "The Pont Neuf Cave" by French street artist JR on the Pont Neuf bridge, May 21, 2026, in Paris.

One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site.

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement.

The oldest bridge in Paris has begun to vanish this week, as the artist JR – who is known as the “French Banksy” – began inflating a giant “cave” over the Pont Neuf.

The monumental, rocky illusion is swallowing the 17th-century landmark, which has carried Parisians across the Seine for more than 400 years. By Thursday, it looked as if a prehistoric cliff had risen in the heart of the city.

The inflation process, which was carried out overnight – after being delayed by bad weather — is the most dramatic stage yet of a project more than a year in the making.

One of the most ambitious public artworks Paris has seen in decades, which has been funded by the sale of JR’s work and a handful of corporate partners, does not open to the public until June 6.

“We’re about to leave something pretty incredible in the middle of Paris,” JR told The Associated Press earlier this year at his studio in the city’s east, wearing his trademark hat and shades.

French street artist JR prepares the inflation of his artwork "The Pont Neuf Cave" on Pont Neuf bridge, May 20, 2026, in Paris, ahead of its free public opening from June 6 to 28. © Thomas Padilla, AP

The transformation of the bridge has been documented by the AP since March with time-lapse cameras, including one fixed on a rooftop terrace high above the river, watching the bridge slowly disappear day by day.

From the outside, the installation looks like a rocky mass that “literally” breaks the landscape, said JR, who is famous for pasting enormous photographs on buildings, walls and rooftops around the world. This time he wanted Parisians to do something unusual on their busiest bridge: stop.

Visitors will be able to walk for free through a long, dark tunnel that lets in no daylight and where, according to JR, people “will lose track of time.”

The numbers are startling. The structure is 120 metres (393 feet) long and 18 meters (59 feet) tall – which is as high as a six-story building.

Yet it is built almost entirely from air – 80 fabric arches filled with 20,000 cubic metres of it – and weighs only about five tons. The fabric was hand stitched by 25 artisans in a village in Brittany.

Nothing digs into the historic stone.

Cut the air and the cliff would sink like a held breath – a collapse JR’s engineers spent weeks rehearsing in a hangar at Orly airport to be sure that if the power ever failed, the rock would come down gently.

The artwork, called La Caverne du Pont Neuf, is a tribute to a Parisian artistic legend.

In 1985, artist Christo and his wife, Jeanne-Claude, wrapped the same bridge in pale golden fabric – 13 kilometres of rope, a decade of arguing with city hall, three million visitors in two weeks. The act helped invent the idea of monumental art in modern cities.

A square beside the bridge now carries their names.

“It’s pretty hard to go after them,” JR said.

His idea, he said, is to bring “mineral and nature” back to the heart of the city. He is not covering the bridge but undressing it – sending the dressed stone back to the limestone quarries from which Paris itself was cut.

The cave is also a warning. JR built it as a nod to Plato’s allegory, in which prisoners mistake shadows on a wall for the real world.

“What are our caves today? Our phones,” he said. “Because we believe that our algorithm on social media is the reality.”

Then he walks straight into the contradiction: to enter his cave about screens, visitors raise their phones.

The tech company Snap has built an augmented-reality layer that shows what the eye cannot.

The sound is a low, mineral hum from Thomas Bangalter, formerly of Daft Punk – who was 10 the year Christo wrapped the bridge.

The cave will be open around the clock from June 6-28, closing the bridge to traffic and visible from the quays, from passing boats, even from the top of the Eiffel Tower.

It will coincide with Paris Fashion Week, World Music Day and the all-night Nuit Blanche arts festival.

When it comes down, the fabric will be reused or recycled. Air, JR likes to say, leaves no scar.

Then, like the golden wrapping 40 years before, the cave will be gone – and the Pont Neuf, older than the republic and older than the revolution, will reappear exactly as it was.

(FRANCE 24 with AP)
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article describes a large-scale, innovative public art installation in Paris that transforms a historic landmark, engaging the public in a meaningful cultural experience while honoring artistic heritage and promoting environmental consciousness through reusable materials. It has broad public significance and emotional uplift, though it is primarily cultural rather than fitting into health, environment, or technology categories.
articles.personality_title: French artist JR wraps Paris’s oldest bridge in giant inflatable cave
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Pont Neuf is the oldest bridge in Paris, built in the 1600s. It is a famous landmark that has been part of the city for over 400 years. In 1985, artists Christo and Jeanne-Claude wrapped the bridge in fabric, creating a well-known public art project.

**What happened** – French street artist JR, known as the “French Banksy,” created a large inflatable sculpture called "La Caverne du Pont Neuf" around the bridge. Starting in May 2026, JR covered the Pont Neuf with a giant fake cave made from fabric filled with air. The structure is 120 meters long and 18 meters tall but weighs only about five tons because it is mostly air. The artwork opens to the public for free from June 6 to June 28.

**Impact** – This art project is unique because it combines history, art, and nature in a surprising way. Instead of hiding the bridge, JR’s cave makes people stop and explore the space differently. Inside, visitors walk through a dark tunnel where they can lose track of time. The project also honors the earlier wrapping by Christo and Jeanne-Claude, connecting two generations of Parisian art. It uses reusable materials, so it won’t damage the historic bridge or the environment.

**What's next step** – The cave will be open to visitors for about three weeks, during which the bridge will be closed to traffic. People can see the artwork from nearby streets, boats, and even the Eiffel Tower. After the display ends, the cave will be taken down carefully, and the materials will be recycled or reused. This project may inspire more creative public art in cities worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The oldest bridge in Paris is temporarily transformed by JR’s giant inflatable cave, inviting people to experience history and art in a new and surprising way.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’artiste français JR enveloppe le plus vieux pont de Paris dans une grotte gonflable géante
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Pont Neuf est le plus ancien pont de Paris, construit au 17e siècle. C’est un monument célèbre qui existe depuis plus de 400 ans. En 1985, les artistes Christo et Jeanne-Claude avaient déjà enveloppé ce pont dans un tissu, créant une œuvre d’art publique très connue.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’artiste de rue français JR, surnommé le « Banksy français », a créé une grande sculpture gonflable appelée « La Caverne du Pont Neuf » autour du pont. À partir de mai 2026, JR a recouvert le Pont Neuf d’une énorme fausse grotte faite de tissu rempli d’air. La structure mesure 120 mètres de long et 18 mètres de haut, mais ne pèse qu’environ cinq tonnes car elle est presque entièrement remplie d’air. L’œuvre est ouverte gratuitement au public du 6 au 28 juin.

**Impact** – Ce projet artistique est unique car il mêle histoire, art et nature de façon surprenante. Au lieu de cacher le pont, la grotte de JR invite les gens à s’arrêter et à découvrir l’espace autrement. À l’intérieur, les visiteurs traversent un tunnel sombre où ils peuvent perdre la notion du temps. Le projet rend aussi hommage à l’enveloppement précédent de Christo et Jeanne-Claude, reliant deux générations d’art parisien. Il utilise des matériaux réutilisables et ne abîme ni le pont ni l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – La grotte sera accessible au public pendant environ trois semaines, durant lesquelles le pont sera fermé à la circulation. Les visiteurs pourront voir l’œuvre depuis les rues proches, les bateaux et même la Tour Eiffel. Une fois l’exposition terminée, la grotte sera démontée avec soin et les matériaux seront recyclés ou réutilisés. Ce projet pourrait inspirer d’autres œuvres d’art publiques créatives dans le monde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le plus vieux pont de Paris est temporairement transformé par la grotte gonflable géante de JR, invitant chacun à vivre l’histoire et l’art d’une manière nouvelle et surprenante.
articles.personality_title_es: El artista francés JR envuelve el puente más antiguo de París en una cueva inflable gigante
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Pont Neuf es el puente más antiguo de París, construido en el siglo XVII. Es un monumento famoso que ha existido por más de 400 años. En 1985, los artistas Christo y Jeanne-Claude envolvieron el puente con tela, creando una conocida obra de arte pública.

**Qué pasó** – El artista callejero francés JR, conocido como el “Banksy francés”, creó una gran escultura inflable llamada "La Caverna del Pont Neuf" alrededor del puente. Desde mayo de 2026, JR cubrió el Pont Neuf con una enorme cueva falsa hecha de tela llena de aire. La estructura mide 120 metros de largo y 18 metros de alto, pero pesa solo unas cinco toneladas porque está casi completamente llena de aire. La obra estará abierta al público gratis del 6 al 28 de junio.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto artístico es único porque combina historia, arte y naturaleza de una manera sorprendente. En lugar de ocultar el puente, la cueva de JR invita a las personas a detenerse y explorar el espacio de forma diferente. Dentro, los visitantes caminan por un túnel oscuro donde pueden perder la noción del tiempo. El proyecto también rinde homenaje al envoltorio anterior de Christo y Jeanne-Claude, conectando dos generaciones de arte parisino. Usa materiales reutilizables, por lo que no dañará el puente histórico ni el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – La cueva estará abierta a los visitantes durante unas tres semanas, tiempo durante el cual el puente estará cerrado al tráfico. La gente podrá ver la obra desde las calles cercanas, los barcos e incluso desde la Torre Eiffel. Cuando termine la exhibición, la cueva se desmontará cuidadosamente y los materiales se reciclarán o reutilizarán. Este proyecto podría inspirar más arte público creativo en ciudades de todo el mundo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El puente más antiguo de París se transforma temporalmente con la cueva inflable gigante de JR, invitando a la gente a experimentar la historia y el arte de una manera nueva y sorprendente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a grand, ancient stone bridge gently enveloped by a vast, translucent inflatable cave structure made of soft, rocky fabric arches, floating lightly above a calm river beneath a pastel sky, with subtle glowing silhouettes of visitors entering a dark tunnel inside the cave, evoking a harmonious blend of history, art, and ephemeral nature.
